turns.content: I built a simple parallel processor using SharedArrayBuffer and wasm-bindgen-rayon, but I'm getting a runtime error when trying to access the buffer from different threads.

That's frustrating because you've likely hit the browser restriction on SharedArrayBuffer which requires StrictHostedContext. The specific issue is that wasm-bindgen-rayon can only operate correctly if atomics are properly configured for your thread model, otherwise you'll see race conditions when multiple threads try to write to the same memory region.
I thought I just needed to add the crate and set up my environment variables. Can't SharedArrayBuffer handle this automatically?

SharedArrayBuffer is designed for single-threaded parallelism within the browser context, not multi-threading across JavaScript threads or WebAssembly threads. The crate provides a safer abstraction but doesn't bypass the browser's security model which enforces atomic operations via the Memory type in your wasm module.
So I can't actually use multiple threads with SharedArrayBuffer?

You cannot spawn actual OS-level threads in a browser environment because the WebAssembly thread model is restricted by the browser's security policy. Even if you try to simulate parallelism using rayon, the underlying memory access patterns must respect the atomic constraints enforced by the SharedArrayBuffer specification.
Ah, I see. So instead of spawning threads, I should just use a single thread with proper synchronization primitives like Mutex or atomic operations to manage shared state?

Exactly. You need to ensure all your parallel logic runs on a single logical thread while coordinating access through atomics or locks, which is the only safe pattern for SharedArrayBuffer in this context.

I've been trying to run some heavy parallel computations in the browser and I keep hitting a wall because of thread restrictions. Is there any way to actually get multi-threaded execution inside a WebAssembly module without falling back to single-threaded JS?

The good news is that you can use shared memory via SharedArrayBuffer which lets threads talk to each other, but the real magic comes from how you manage synchronization. You'll want to look at atomics in WASM specifically using the wasm-atomics crate or the standard library's atomic types like AtomicU32 and AtomicBool. These let different threads safely read and write the same memory location without race conditions.

That sounds promising but I'm worried about browser compatibility since SharedArrayBuffer isn't supported everywhere yet. Are there alternatives to rayon for parallelism that work better with these constraints?

For now, the most practical approach is combining SharedArrayBuffer with wasm-bindgen-rayon which provides a Rayonic worker thread interface tailored for WebAssembly. You can spawn rayon workers that push tasks onto a queue stored in shared memory using atomic compare-exchange operations. This pattern lets you get true parallelism while respecting browser limits on thread count and memory sharing.
Hey, I'm trying to parallelize a data processing job in the browser using SharedArrayBuffer and wasm-bindgen-rayon, but the docs make it sound like I can just spawn threads like in normal Rust.

I can see why that feels off. The SharedArrayBuffer doesn't actually support traditional OS threads or std::thread. You need to use the WebAssembly thread model which is different from how you'd do it on Linux. Instead of creating threads, you rely on shared memory access patterns and synchronization primitives.
So there's no way to actually run multiple worker processes in the browser? That seems like a huge limitation compared to server-side Rust where I can just drop std::thread into my lib.rs file.

That is exactly right. In WASM you cannot use std::thread or any multi-threading library because the browser environment doesn't allow it. You have to work with atomics and synchronization primitives that are defined in the WebAssembly standard itself.
Okay, so for my SharedArrayBuffer I need to use something like AtomicU32 instead of regular integers? And how does wasm-bindgen-rayon actually help me if there are no real threads?

You're using atomics correctly. The SharedArrayBuffer interface requires all access to be synchronized via atomic operations like AtomicU32 or AtomicU64. wasm-bindgen-rayon provides a Rayoned trait that lets you write parallel code, but it's more about managing the shared memory state than spawning actual threads.
Wait, so I'm not actually using rayon's multi-threading capabilities here? Like when I call .par_iter() on a Vec in normal Rust it spawns multiple workers automatically?

Exactly. In standard Rust your par_iter() uses std::thread to create actual worker processes. In WASM, the Rayoned trait lets you express parallelism but relies on the browser's single-threaded execution model with careful synchronization of the SharedArrayBuffer.
This is frustrating because I wrote a lot of code assuming thread safety in my normal Rust library, and now it doesn't even compile when I try to port it to WASM. What's actually blocking me?

The main blocker is that SharedArrayBuffer requires all memory accesses to be atomic and properly synchronized. Your normal Rust code probably has regular reads and writes which won't work here. You need to replace those with Atomic operations and ensure your Rayoned functions respect the synchronization requirements.
So I can't just drop my existing parallel logic in? What's a concrete example of what I should write instead for that SharedArrayBuffer scenario?

Instead of spawning threads, you'd implement a Rayoned function that iterates through your data and uses Atomic operations to update the SharedArrayBuffer. The key is ensuring every read-write sequence on that buffer goes through atomics so there are no race conditions in the browser environment.
